contradiction_text: High aggregate evaluator accuracy does not remove the risk that the specific wrong errors most harmful to refinement remain present, because the paper says false negatives damage Reflexion more than false positives.
related_claim_texts: Across the paper's web and device-control benchmarks, the automatic evaluators matched oracle metrics or human judgments with reported accuracy ranging from 74.4% to 92.9%.
The paper identifies false-negative evaluator judgments as more damaging to Reflexion than false positives because they cause unnecessary retries of trajectories that were already successful.
disclosure: Strong average agreement should not be presented as sufficient on its own; the paper's own mechanism claim requires keeping visible that error type, not just error rate, determines whether evaluator-guided refinement helps or harms.

contradiction_text: The article presents one accuracy range across web and device-control benchmarks, but the underlying reference standard changes across domains, with WebArena measured against an oracle evaluator and Android-in-the-Wild measured against human judgment.
related_claim_texts: Across the paper's web and device-control benchmarks, the automatic evaluators matched oracle metrics or human judgments with reported accuracy ranging from 74.4% to 92.9%.
disclosure: The cross-domain accuracy band should remain visibly non-uniform, because it combines agreement against different reference types rather than one fully commensurate standard of correctness.

contradiction_text: The Android evidence leans on human judgment as the comparison target even though the sampled Android-in-the-Wild set includes a substantial share of unsuccessful human demonstrations, which complicates any easy reading of human-produced traces as clean supervision.
related_claim_texts: Across the paper's web and device-control benchmarks, the automatic evaluators matched oracle metrics or human judgments with reported accuracy ranging from 74.4% to 92.9%.
disclosure: The draft should keep visible that Android evaluation is anchored to human judgment in a setting where human demonstrations themselves are not uniformly successful, so reference quality and evaluator quality should not be collapsed into the same claim.

contradiction_text: The iOS result is framed as a 75% relative improvement, but the absolute outcome remains modest, rising from 8 to 14 completed tasks on 52 held-out tasks.
related_claim_texts: On the paper's iOS test set, reward-filtered behavior cloning improved CogAgent from 8 completed tasks at baseline to 14 on 52 held-out tasks, which the authors describe as a 75% relative improvement.
disclosure: The large relative gain should not obscure the still-limited absolute task completion level; the paper supports meaningful improvement, not broad robustness.
